questions.question:
The main memory of a computer has 2^n blocks while the cache has 2^k blocks. If the cache uses the set associative mapping scheme with 2^r blocks per set, then block j of the main memory maps to the set:
A. (j mod 2^n) of the cache
B. (j mod 2^k) of the cache
C. (j mod 2^(k-r)) of the cache
D. (j mod 2^r) of the cache
questions.to_find: Which set does main memory block j map to in a set-associative cache?

understand.plain: The cache has 2^k blocks organized into sets of 2^r blocks each, giving 2^k / 2^r = 2^(k-r) sets. In set-associative mapping, a main memory block maps to exactly one set, determined by taking the block number modulo the total number of sets.

keywords.explain: Cache mapping where the cache is divided into sets, each containing multiple ways (blocks). A memory block maps to a fixed set but can occupy any way within that set.
keywords.example: 4-way set associative cache with 8 sets: block 13 maps to set 13 mod 8 = 5

keywords.explain: Total cache blocks divided by blocks per set gives the number of sets.
keywords.example: Cache with 2^k=32 blocks and 2^r=4 blocks/set has 32/4=8 sets

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' viewBox='0 0 480 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='140' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Main Memory</text><rect x='10' y='50' width='140' height='20' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='64' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2^n blocks (0..2^n-1)</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='140' height='20' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='89' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Block j</text><line x1='150' y1='85' x2='220' y2='85' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='185' y='78' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>j mod 2^(k-r)</text><rect x='220' y='10' width='240' height='170' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Cache (2^k blocks)</text><rect x='230' y='40' width='220' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Set 0 (2^r blocks/set)</text><rect x='230' y='75' width='220' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='95' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Set j mod 2^(k-r)</text><rect x='230' y='110' width='220' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='130' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>... (2^(k-r) sets total)</text><text x='340' y='165' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Each set has 2^r blocks</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Main memory block j maps to cache set number (j mod 2^(k-r)), where the cache has 2^(k-r) sets each containing 2^r blocks.

given.aim: Find the set number in cache to which main memory block j maps using set-associative mapping.

terms.meaning: Total number of blocks in main memory
terms.example: If n=20, main memory has 1M blocks
terms.connects: Determines the address space but not directly the mapping set

terms.meaning: Total number of blocks in the cache
terms.connects: Used together with 2^r to compute number of sets

given.plan: Compute the number of sets = 2^k / 2^r = 2^(k-r). In set-associative mapping the set index for block j is j mod (number of sets) = j mod 2^(k-r).

steps.title: Calculate the number of sets in the cache
steps.formula_raw: Number of sets = Total cache blocks / Blocks per set = 2^k / 2^r = 2^(k-r)
steps.apply: Cache has 2^k blocks; each set holds 2^r blocks. So total sets = 2^(k-r).
steps.note: This is a key formula: sets = 2^(k-r). The r bits select the way (block within set), and k-r bits select the set.

steps.title: Determine which set block j maps to
steps.formula_raw: Set index = j mod (Number of sets) = j mod 2^(k-r)
steps.apply: Block j of main memory maps to set (j mod 2^(k-r)) of the cache. This is because set-associative mapping uses the low-order (k-r) bits of the block number as the set index.
steps.note: The answer matches option C. Options A and B are wrong (they use 2^n or 2^k, not the number of sets). Option D uses 2^r which is blocks per set, not number of sets.

solution.result: Block j of main memory maps to set (j mod 2^(k-r)) of the cache — Option C.

questions.formula_note: Set-associative cache mapping: set index = (memory block number) mod (number of sets); number of sets = total cache blocks / ways per set = 2^(k-r).

questions.question:
More than one word are put in one cache block to:
A. exploit the temporal locality of reference in a program
B. exploit the spatial locality of reference in a program
C. reduce the miss penalty
D. none of the above
questions.answer: B. exploit the spatial locality of reference in a program
questions.to_find: Why are multiple words placed in a single cache block?

understand.plain: When a cache miss occurs, the CPU fetches not just the needed word but an entire block (multiple consecutive words) from main memory. This is done because programs tend to access memory locations that are close to recently-used locations — a principle called spatial locality. By loading nearby words together, future accesses to those neighbors will likely be cache hits.

keywords.explain: The tendency of programs to access memory locations that are physically near recently accessed locations.
keywords.example: Accessing array[0], then array[1], array[2] — consecutive addresses

keywords.explain: The tendency of programs to reuse recently accessed memory locations soon again.
keywords.example: A loop variable i is read/written many times in quick succession

understand.visual_alt: A cache block holds multiple consecutive words (e.g., 16 bytes). When word at address X is needed, words at X+1, X+2, ... are also loaded — exploiting spatial locality.

given.aim: Explain the motivation for having multi-word cache blocks.

terms.meaning: The unit of data transfer between cache and main memory, containing multiple consecutive words.
terms.example: A 64-byte cache line holds 16 words of 4 bytes each
terms.connects: Larger blocks exploit spatial locality by prefetching neighbors

terms.meaning: Programs tend to access memory near recently used addresses.
terms.connects: Loading an entire block amortizes transfer cost across likely-needed neighbors

terms.meaning: Time wasted to fetch a block from main memory on a cache miss.
terms.example: If memory latency is 100 ns and block transfer takes 150 ns total, penalty = 150 ns
terms.connects: Larger blocks do NOT reduce miss penalty; they can increase it

given.plan: Analyze what benefit loading multiple words together provides: it prefetches neighboring data that programs are likely to need next (spatial locality), not temporal locality or miss penalty reduction.

steps.title: Eliminate temporal locality (option A)
steps.apply: Temporal locality is exploited by the cache itself (keeping recently used blocks in cache). The block size (number of words per block) does not affect temporal reuse — a single-word block would still exploit temporal locality. So option A is incorrect.
steps.note: Caching at all exploits temporal locality; block size exploits spatial locality.

steps.title: Evaluate spatial locality (option B)
steps.apply: When one word is needed, the CPU fetches its entire block. Neighbouring words in the block are likely to be needed soon (array traversal, sequential instruction execution). Loading them proactively turns future misses into hits — this is spatial locality exploitation.
steps.note: This is the correct and well-known rationale for multi-word cache blocks.

steps.title: Eliminate miss penalty (option C)
steps.apply: Larger blocks actually increase (or at best do not reduce) miss penalty, because more data must be transferred on each miss. So option C is incorrect.
steps.note: Miss penalty is reduced by techniques like early restart or critical-word-first, not by larger blocks.

solution.result: Multiple words per cache block exploit spatial locality of reference — Option B.

questions.formula_note: Spatial locality principle: programs access nearby memory addresses sequentially; loading a full block on a miss pre-loads likely-needed neighbors.

questions.question:
A CPU has a 32-bit memory address and a 4 KB cache memory. The cache is organized as a 4-way set associative cache with a cache block size of 16 bytes.
(A) What is the number of sets in the cache?
(B) What is the size (in bits) of the tag field per cache block?
(C) What is the number and size of comparators required for tag matching on a cache read?
questions.answer: A: 64 sets; B: 22 bits; C: 4 comparators, each 22 bits wide
questions.to_find: Number of sets, tag field size, and comparators needed for tag matching in this 4-way set associative cache.

understand.plain: Given a 32-bit address space and a 4KB, 4-way set-associative cache with 16-byte blocks, we need to partition the 32-bit address into offset, index, and tag fields. The block offset bits address bytes within a block, the set index bits select the set, and the remaining bits form the tag. The number of sets equals total cache blocks divided by the associativity. On a read, all 4 ways in a set are checked simultaneously, so 4 comparators (one per way) are needed, each as wide as the tag.

keywords.explain: Each set contains 4 ways (blocks). A memory block can be stored in any of the 4 ways of its designated set.
The upper portion of a memory address stored in the cache to verify whether a cached block matches the requested address.
keywords.example: 32-bit address: [tag 22 bits | set index 6 bits | block offset 4 bits]

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='160' viewBox='0 0 500 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='35' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Tag (22 bits)</text><rect x='230' y='10' width='130' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='295' y='35' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Set Index (6 bits)</text><rect x='360' y='10' width='130' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='425' y='35' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Block Offset (4 bits)</text><text x='10' y='75' class='sub mut'>Bit 31</text><text x='225' y='75' class='sub mut'>Bit 10</text><text x='355' y='75' class='sub mut'>Bit 4</text><text x='460' y='75' class='sub mut'>Bit 0</text><text x='250' y='110' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>32-bit address partitioned into Tag | Set Index | Block Offset</text><text x='250' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>On read: 4 comparators (one per way) each compare 22-bit tag</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit address split into: [Tag 22 bits | Set Index 6 bits | Block Offset 4 bits]. 4 comparators check all 4 ways in the matched set simultaneously.

given.aim: Compute cache parameters: number of sets, tag bits, and comparator requirements for a 4KB, 4-way set associative cache with 16-byte blocks and 32-bit addresses.

terms.meaning: Total data capacity of the cache is 4 * 1024 = 4096 bytes.
terms.connects: Total blocks / ways per set = number of sets

terms.meaning: Each cache block holds 16 bytes; needs log_2(16) = 4 bits for block offset.
terms.connects: Determines offset field width in address

terms.meaning: Each set has 4 ways (blocks). Total sets = total blocks / 4.
terms.connects: Determines number of comparators (one per way) needed on a read

terms.meaning: Each memory address is 32 bits wide.
terms.connects: Determines tag field width, which sets comparator width

given.plan: Step 1: find total cache blocks. Step 2: find number of sets = total blocks / 4 ways. Step 3: compute offset bits = log_2(block size), set-index bits = log_2(number of sets), tag bits = 32 - index - offset. Step 4: comparators = ways = 4, each of width = tag bits.

steps.title: Compute total number of cache blocks and sets
steps.formula_raw: Total blocks = Cache size / Block size; Sets = Total blocks / Ways
steps.apply:
Total blocks = 4096 bytes / 16 bytes = 256 blocks.
Sets = 256 / 4 = 64 sets.
steps.note: 64 = 2^6, so 6 bits are needed for the set index.

steps.title: Determine address field widths
steps.formula_raw: Offset bits = log_2(block size); Index bits = log_2(sets); Tag bits = total address bits - index bits - offset bits
steps.apply:
Block offset bits = log_2(16) = 4 bits.
Set index bits = log_2(64) = 6 bits.
Tag bits = 32 - 6 - 4 = 22 bits.
steps.note: The 32-bit address is partitioned as: [Tag: 22 bits | Set index: 6 bits | Offset: 4 bits].

steps.title: Determine comparators for tag matching
steps.formula_raw: Comparators needed = Associativity (ways per set); Comparator width = tag bits
steps.apply: On a cache read, the 6-bit set index selects one set. All 4 ways in that set must be checked simultaneously to find a hit. Therefore, 4 comparators are needed, each 22 bits wide.
steps.note: Parallel comparison in all ways is what makes set-associative cache fast; sequential comparison would add latency.

solution.result: (A) 64 sets; (B) 22 bits per tag field; (C) 4 comparators, each 22 bits wide.

questions.formula_note: Cache address partitioning: offset = log_2(block size), index = log_2(number of sets), tag = total bits - index - offset; comparators = ways per set (one per way, width = tag bits).

questions.question:
In a C program, an array is declared as float A[2048]. Each array element is 4 Bytes in size, and the starting address of the array is 0x00000000. This program is run on a computer that has a direct mapped data cache of size 8 KB, with block (line) size of 16 Bytes.
(A) Which elements of the array conflict with element A[0] in the data cache? Justify your answer briefly.
(B) If the program accesses the elements of this array one by one in reverse order, i.e., starting with the last element and ending with the first element, how many data cache misses will occur? Justify your answer briefly. Assume that the data cache is initially empty and that no other data or instruction accesses are to be considered.
questions.answer: A: A[2048] is out of range; elements that conflict with A[0] are A[2048] (if it existed) — actually A[k*2048] for integer k (since cache holds 2 KB = 512 elements; 2048/512=4 groups). Conflicts: A[2048] does not exist; correct answer: every element at index that is a multiple of 2048 would conflict, but within array bounds the conflicting elements are none except those that map to the same cache block set. Specifically, elements A[0] through A[3] share the first cache block; elements that conflict with A[0]'s block are A[2048] (out of range). So no element within the array conflicts with A[0] because array size equals cache size. For part B: 512 misses (one per cache block for the 512 blocks in 8KB cache).
questions.to_find: (A) Which array elements conflict with A[0] in the direct-mapped cache. (B) Total cache misses for a reverse-order traversal of the array.

understand.plain: The array has 2048 floats * 4 bytes = 8192 bytes total. The cache is 8 KB = 8192 bytes. Each cache block is 16 bytes holding 4 floats. Since the array is exactly the same size as the cache, the entire array fits in the cache with no conflict. In a direct-mapped cache, conflicts happen only when two memory blocks map to the same cache block. Since the array fits exactly once, no element conflicts with A[0]. For reverse traversal, each new block is accessed for the first time causing a compulsory miss — there are 8192/16 = 512 blocks total, so 512 misses.

keywords.explain: Each memory block maps to exactly one cache block (no choice). Conflicts occur when two different memory blocks map to the same cache slot.
keywords.example: Cache of 8KB with 16B blocks has 512 slots. Memory block i maps to slot i mod 512.

keywords.explain: A miss caused by two memory blocks competing for the same cache slot, evicting each other.
keywords.example: In an 8KB direct-mapped cache, A[0] (at address 0) and a hypothetical A[2048] (at address 8192) would map to same slot.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='200' viewBox='0 0 500 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='460' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='35' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Array A: 2048 floats = 8192 bytes = 8 KB</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='460' height='40' class='ok' style='fill-opacity:0.3'/><text x='240' y='85' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Cache: 8 KB direct-mapped, 512 blocks of 16B</text><text x='240' y='120' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Array size == Cache size: entire array maps 1-to-1</text><text x='240' y='145' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>No two elements in A[0..2047] conflict with each other</text><rect x='10' y='155' width='220' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='177' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>A[0]..A[3] → cache block 0</text><rect x='240' y='155' width='230' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='355' y='177' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>A[2044]..A[2047] → cache block 511</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The 8KB array maps exactly onto the 8KB cache with no overlap. Each 16-byte cache block holds 4 floats. 512 cache blocks total, so 512 compulsory misses for a full traversal.

given.aim: Find conflicting elements with A[0] and count cache misses for reverse-order array traversal.

terms.term: Array A[2048], 4 bytes per element, base address 0
terms.meaning: Total array size = 2048 * 4 = 8192 bytes = 8 KB, occupying addresses 0x0000 to 0x1FFF.
terms.connects: Determines which cache blocks each element maps to

terms.meaning: Cache has 8192/16 = 512 blocks; memory block i maps to cache block (i mod 512).
terms.example: Memory block 0 (addresses 0-15) maps to cache block 0; memory block 512 (addresses 8192-8207) also maps to cache block 0
terms.connects: A[0] is in memory block 0 → cache block 0; next conflicting block is memory block 512 = address 8192 = A[2048] which is out of array bounds

terms.meaning: Accessing A[2047], A[2046], ..., A[1], A[0] — each element accessed exactly once.
terms.example: 2048 accesses total, 4 elements per cache block, so 512 distinct cache blocks touched
terms.connects: Each block is accessed for the first time (cold/compulsory miss), giving 512 total misses

given.plan: Part A: find which elements alias to same cache block as A[0]. Part B: count distinct cache blocks accessed during reverse traversal.

steps.title: Part A — Identify conflicting elements with A[0]
steps.formula_raw: Cache block index = (memory address / block size) mod (number of cache blocks)
steps.apply: Cache has 8192/16 = 512 cache blocks (indexed 0 to 511). A[0] is at address 0; its memory block number = 0/16 = 0; cache block = 0 mod 512 = 0. The next memory block mapping to cache block 0 is memory block 512 (at address 512*16 = 8192). Address 8192 corresponds to A[8192/4] = A[2048]. But A[2048] is out of bounds (array indices 0 to 2047). Therefore, no element within the array A[0..2047] conflicts with A[0].
steps.note: The array is exactly 8 KB = cache size, so it maps one-to-one onto the cache with no wrap-around conflicts within the array bounds.

steps.title: Part B — Count cache misses for reverse traversal
steps.formula_raw: Misses = Total distinct cache blocks accessed = Array size / Block size
steps.apply: The array has 8192 bytes; each cache block holds 16 bytes; total cache blocks = 8192/16 = 512. In a reverse traversal, we access every element exactly once. Each group of 4 consecutive elements (e.g., A[2044]..A[2047]) is in one cache block. First access to each block is a miss (cache starts empty). No block is evicted before it is done (only 512 blocks fit, and we never revisit). Total misses = 512.
steps.note: All misses are compulsory (cold-start) misses. If the access were sequential (forward), misses would still be 512 for the same reason.

solution.result: (A) No element within A[0..2047] conflicts with A[0] (the next conflicting address 8192 = A[2048] is out of bounds). (B) 512 cache misses occur during the reverse traversal.

questions.formula_note: Direct-mapped cache conflict: two blocks conflict iff (address / block_size) mod num_cache_blocks are equal. Total compulsory misses = array_size / block_size when array fits entirely in cache and cache starts empty.
